Scoreboard

Primary mission

No layout loaded. Load one in Setup to see the primary mission and objectives.

Secondary objectives

Draw from the Tactical deck. Drawn cards are shared — your opponent sees your hand too. Tap 📖 to read a card; card text is editable so you can match your own deck exactly.

Stratagems

Core stratagems for the current phase. Clicking spends CP from your pool (blocked if you can't afford it) — effect text isn't shown here, check your codex/app.

My detachment notes

Type your own detachment stratagem/ability names + short notes here — this is yours, saved only in your browser, never shipped rules text.

Attacker

Target

Set up the profile and roll. Full dice detail is logged for both players.

Battle-shock / Leadership

You

Phones get a board-first layout: one-row top strip, bottom nav, and slide-up sheets for the tabs and log. Applies immediately — no reload.

Battlefield

Terrain & objectives

Layouts are symmetric tournament-style maps scaled to your board. Or place by hand: pick a tool in the toolbar, drag to place terrain, click to drop objectives. Select + R rotates, Del removes.

Rules aids

Dragging models shows the distance moved vs the unit's Move stat — white within M, yellow up to M+6" (advance territory), red beyond. Your opponent sees your drag path live.
Bases may not end a move overlapping a wall or container, and only INFANTRY/BEASTS/SWARM may end inside a ruin footprint.
Unticked, when the AI damages one of your units the game pauses and you allocate each damage packet by clicking your models (an already-wounded model must take them first — the real rule). A auto-assigns the rest. Tick this to go back to fully automatic casualties.
Once a model has moved this phase it can't be re-dragged until the rest of its unit has also moved. Use "Movement complete" in a unit's inspector to lock it early, or "↩ Undo move" to snap it back to where the phase began. Untick this to drag freely like before.
Models breaking coherency (2" of a squadmate, 9" of every squadmate) glow red. With enforcement on, a drag that breaks a previously-coherent unit is reverted.

Visuals

Cosmetic only. The red/blue base rim always shows who owns a model; the body colour is its faction. Untick to go back to plain red-vs-blue tokens.

Game

Unit card
ARMY BUILDER

Roster

Roster auto-saves in your browser. Detachments and enhancements aren't validated — check your faction pack.

Import card text

Paste your cards' text here to load your whole deck at once — it saves locally and shares to your opponent. Use one block per card, each starting with ## Card Name on its own line, followed by that card's text. (Single lines like Card Name :: text also work.)

A brief summary of what this mission rewards. The exact scoring is on your physical card — paste it here to keep it handy, and it'll show for both players.

Secondary objective deck

One card name per line — edit to match the deck you own. Only card names are stored; keep your physical cards for the scoring rules.

Import army list

Paste an army list (official app export or similar). I'll detect the faction, match every unit to the database, and build your cards — stats, weapons, bases and model counts included.

⚔ Solo vs AI

The AI builds a list, deploys into the Blue (side 2) deployment zone and plays its own turns through the phase stepper. You play Red (side 1) and take the first turn. Load a terrain layout first so it has a deployment zone and objectives. See ? → Solo mode for what it does (and approximates).

How to play online

  1. One of you clicks Host and reads out the room code (also copied to your clipboard).
  2. The other opens this same file and clicks Join, entering the code. You each need this HTML file — just send it over.
  3. Set your name and side in Setup. Everything on the table, the trackers, dice and the log are shared live.

The table

  • Select tool: drag models to move them. Drag on empty space for a box-select; drag any selected model to move the group. Del removes, R rotates vehicles/terrain.
  • With models selected, press 1–4 to toggle range rings (6"/9"/12"/24"). Click a model to see its unit; use ± on the unit card to track wounds.
  • Model actions menu: long-press a model (touch) or right-click it (mouse) — or tap ⋯ on the inspector — for wounds, role/weapon marking, Hidden, floor level, battle-shock, attach, rotate, reserves and delete. Everything in it also syncs to your opponent.
  • Model markings: squad leaders (sergeants, nobs, superiors…) deploy with a gold base rim and chevron automatically. Special/heavy weapons get a colour-coded letter pip at the base's bottom edge — auto-detected from your Builder loadout notes where possible, or set via the menu (Role / weapon tag). W still sets a free-text tag under the base. Legend:
  • Tape measure: drag to measure in inches — your opponent sees your measurement too.
  • Builder (full screen): build a points-legal roster — pick faction and points limit, click unit cards to add them, change sizes, watch the total. Muster army puts every unit on the table in one click and returns you to the game. Esc closes it. Export the list as text to share it.
  • Army tab: every faction's units are pre-loaded — pick your faction, click a unit to add its card (stats, weapons, bases, points), or Import army list to paste a list and get your whole army on the table in one go. Cards save in your browser.
  • Coherency: models breaking unit coherency glow red and moves that break a coherent unit snap back (toggle in Setup).
  • Terrain layouts: Setup tab → pick a tournament-style layout → Load. Objectives included.
  • Cards tab: a shared scoreboard (VP/CP/round, synced with the top bar), the current primary mission, and a Tactical secondary deck you draw from — both players see each other's hands. Tap 📖 on any card to read its summary; the text is editable so you can paste the exact scoring from the deck you own, and it syncs to your opponent.
  • Attack tab: enter the weapon profile and target; it rolls the whole 11th-edition sequence (crits, Sustained, Lethal, Dev Wounds, Anti-X, Twin-linked, invulns, FNP) and logs the dice for both players.
  • Save game (Setup tab) downloads the whole table state; either player can reload it later.
  • Line of sight — 👁 tool: click one of your units to see a line to every enemy unit: green = visible, yellow = visible with Benefit of Cover (tick Cover in the Attack tab: −1 to hit), red = not visible (or Hidden beyond 15"). Visibility is 11th-ed area style: a target only counts as out of sight when every sight line crosses a ruin/wood/container/low-wall area, ignoring any area either model is inside — craters never block. Approximations used: sight lines are sampled centre-to-centre plus 4 base-edge points each way; cover = a sight line clipped by an area, or an INFANTRY/BEASTS/SWARM model standing inside one. Select a unit and press H to toggle Hidden for quiet infantry sitting wholly in dense terrain (ruin/wood/container) — while hidden it reads as not visible beyond 15". The tool advises; you two still apply the exact rules.
  • Terrain physicality: a model can't end a move with its base overlapping a low wall or container, and only INFANTRY/BEASTS/SWARM can end inside a ruin footprint — illegal drops snap back (own "impassable terrain" toggle in Setup, on by default; deploys that land badly just warn). Select models standing in a ruin and press F to cycle floor level 0–3 (INFANTRY/BEASTS/SWARM/FLY only): the ▲ badge shows the level, each level counts 3" of movement, and a raised model sees over low walls when checking line of sight. Approximations: wall/container overlap uses the base circle; "inside a ruin" is judged by base centre.
  • Turn sequence & reserves: the top-bar phase stepper walks Command→Movement→Shooting→Charge→Fight→End for each player in turn. Entering a Command phase gives both players +1 CP automatically (once per turn — the manual CP buttons still work) and logs a battle-shock reminder listing your units at half strength or less. Click one of your units and use the inspector's → Reserves to lift it into the Reserves tray (bottom-left); Deploy from the tray shades where it may arrive — from round 2, within 6" of a battlefield edge and more than 8" from enemy models, keeping out of the enemy deployment zone before round 3, or anywhere more than 8" away with the DS box ticked. Shift-click places anyway (logged) if you two agree. Select a CHARACTER and another of your units (shift-click) and press A (or the inspector's Attach) to attach them — they then move, check coherency and count OC as one unit; A again (or Detach) splits them back to their original units.

Solo mode

  • ⚔ Solo (top bar, offline only) starts a practice game against a local AI that plays Blue (side 2). Pick its faction and points; it builds a list from the database (1–2 characters, battleline, a heavy or two, infantry fill — no enhancements), deploys legally in its zone, attaches its characters to squads, and plays every phase of its own turns through the normal phase stepper. You play Red, take the first turn, and play your turns exactly as usual; press › at your End phase to hand over. ⏸/▶ pauses the AI mid-turn, ⏭ finishes its turn instantly. Click ⚔ Solo again to exit (its models stay put). Solo and network play are mutually exclusive.
  • What it plays: it scores objectives each turn (same OC math as the markers), moves units to take/hold/contest them, keeps shooters in cover and out of charge range, shoots with the real Attack-tab dice sequence at targets in range and line of sight, attempts profitable charges (real 2D6), tests battle-shock for its half-strength units (real 2D6 vs Ld), and holds a unit in Strategic Reserves when it can. Every action logs a one-line WHY. It reads only what a player could see: the board and both sides' unit cards — no hidden information, no fudged dice.
  • Solo auto-casualties: in solo mode the AI's damage is applied automatically, and when you roll a staged attack (inspector ⚔ flow) you're offered the same. Allocation: already-wounded model first, then closest to the attacker, squad leaders and CHARACTERs last; excess damage from one failed save is lost, mortal wounds apply one at a time; total is capped at the rolled result after FNP.
  • Documented approximations: the AI fires its unit's best non-pistol/grenade weapon once per model and every other profile once (the database doesn't say who carries what); the database has no Deep Strike flag, so it uses Strategic Reserves (board-edge arrivals) instead; it spends no CP and uses no stratagems; cover and visibility use the same area-rule approximations as the 👁 tool; charges "pile in" as a straight move into contact — swing your own engaged units back manually in its Fight phase. Its 2D6/advance rolls come from a seedable RNG (aiSeed(n) in the console reproduces a game); attack-sequence dice are the ordinary roller's dice.
  • You allocate your own casualties: when an AI attack damages one of your units, the AI pauses and a banner shows the pending damage packets (one per failed save, mortal wounds one at a time). Click models in the target unit to assign packets — if a model in the unit has already lost wounds it must take them first, so clicks are redirected there. A (or the banner button) auto-assigns the rest exactly the way the automatic mode would (wounded first, closest to the attacker next, leaders last). Esc can't cancel — the damage is owed — but Undo works afterwards, and clearing/loading a game discards pending packets. Tick Auto-apply my casualties in Setup for the old hands-off behaviour. The AI's own casualties stay automatic: it is the defender there and allocates the same way. In a network game the attacker's button sends the rolled damage across and this same banner appears on the defender's screen — gold rings and numbered badges suggest the order auto-assign would use, but the defender clicks whatever legal model they want. Pending damage doesn't survive a reconnect — re-send if that happens.
  • Meta opponents: the Solo dialog can field one of five embedded tournament archetypes (real GT-winning lists from 11th edition's opening weeks — unit names, sizes and points only). Each carries a one-line note on what it stress-tests. The AI reads the list's Force Disposition and announces its game plan at muster, plus a one-line strategic brief each round.
  • The AI's doctrine (in its own words): it plays its plan — kill-focused, objective-first, or spread-and-scout — and pushes the primary hardest in rounds 1–3, easing off late when it is ahead. Quiet infantry sitting wholly in dense terrain goes Hidden after the shooting phase, and fragile shooters prefer spots that keep that option. When you hold reserves or fast melee, its cheap units picket 6–8" ahead of its valuable units and backfield objective to deny drops and charge lanes. Early on, if it is outgunned, shooters stage out of sight rather than trade in the open. It concentrates fire to remove whole units (with a modest overkill allowance) and prefers finishing hurt units that sit on objectives; it won't move a unit into the open when the expected loss beats the gain; and its slow melee refuses to chase faster shooters — it parks on objectives and makes you come.

Rules note: you and your opponent apply the game rules — this table doesn't police them, it just does the rolling and bookkeeping. Points and datasheets: official app / Munitorum Field Manual.

🏆 Meta dashboard — tournament results and army win rates.
